evidence_quotes.quote: The value of the legitimate keys are artificially driven to hundred of euros for nothing
A license to a service can be revoked unilaterally at any point without a refund.

paragraphs.text: Hello, I think the digital fairness act could includes legislation concerning a specific topic that includes hundred of millions of people around the world, video games. A separate initiative is ongoing, the Stop Killing Games initiative with over a million signatures. Nowadays, games tend to be sold at full price so 80 or more. If you dig in the user agreement (that no one actually reads), you are not buying a game but a license to a game which is not the same at all. A license to a service can be revoked unilaterally at any point without a refund. A game you own even if online services are shutdown. This distinction should be clear for the consumer. An effort should be made by game developer and publisher regarding the end of life of their games. They shouldn't have to keep paying to host servers but they should make it easier for someone else to do it. For example, Battlefield Bad Company 2 is a game released around 2010 which is still played nowadays. The game is being "killed" unilaterally, it is no longer possible to legitimately buy a key to the game on platform such as Steam. That is despite of the fact that the game is still playable in solo (offline) and online with private individual hosting servers for people to join. The value of the legitimate keys are artificially driven to hundred of euros for nothing and consumers have no recourse. There is a topic to address here and the Digital Fairness Act could tackle this issue. Best regards.
